claim: The Voynich text encodes a natural language using a null-cipher or homophones, where multiple glyphs map to the same plaintext character, which would explain the high hapax ratio (70.1%) and lower glyph entropy (3.86 bits) relative to expected natural language entropy while preserving Zipf-like structure.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.86 bits is slightly below Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95), consistent with homophonic substitution compressing entropy
Zipf exponent 0.8946 with R-squared 0.9084 indicates moderate but real Zipf fit, consistent with encoded natural language
25 unique glyphs is a small alphabet; homophones would require mapping multiple glyphs to fewer phonemes
evidence_against: Hapax ratio of 70.1% is anomalously high even for homophonic cipher â€” natural language hapax rates rarely exceed 50%
Word entropy 10.45 bits is very high, inconsistent with simple homophonic substitution of Latin or Italian
test: Compute conditional glyph entropy H(g_i | g_{i-1}) across the full corpus and compare to expected conditional entropy for homophonic Latin and Italian ciphers generated by Monte Carlo simulation with 25-symbol alphabets
test_metric: Bigram conditional entropy in bits, compared against simulated homophonic Latin (expected ~3.2-3.5 bits) vs. random text (~3.86 bits)
test_threshold: Conditional entropy below 3.5 bits would support homophonic cipher; above 3.7 bits would argue against structured encoding

tests_run.test: compute conditional glyph entropy h(g_i | g_{i-1}) across the full corpus and compare to expected co
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: Homophonic ciphers were common in 15th-century European cryptography and would explain the apparent entropy reduction from natural language levels without requiring a completely novel encoding scheme. This hypothesis is testable with existing corpus statistics and would significantly narrow the solution space if confirmed.
